text:
**The exponent picture has a sharp LP obstruction.**

Set up the LP in $r_i, s_i$ (log-side lengths) with ordering, hard-regime sign constraints, and every monomial inequality coming from (i)–(iii): Guth's Estimate 1 for all $(j,k,l)$, integrated directional/Plücker for all six target pairs, your first-alternative consequence $r_1+2r_2\ge s_1+s_2+s_3$, and rectangle DGA/rational-homotopy (e.g. $R_1R_2^2R_3^2R_4\ge S_1S_2^2S_3^2S_4$). Your stress vector
$$r=(-\tfrac15,\tfrac{13}{15},\tfrac{13}{15},\tfrac{13}{15}),\qquad s=(0,0,1,1)$$
is feasible with $\sum r_i=12/5<5/2=T_{\mathrm{exp}}$. The only binding constraints are
$$(D)\; 3r_1+r_2+r_3+r_4\ge 3s_1+s_2+s_3+s_4,\quad (Sl)\; r_1+2(r_2+r_3+r_4)\ge s_1+s_2+3s_3+2s_4.$$
LP duality (multipliers $\lambda_D=\tfrac15,\lambda_{Sl}=\tfrac25$) gives the tight optimum
$$\textstyle\sum r_i\;\ge\;s_1+\tfrac{3s_2+7s_3}{5}+s_4\;=\;T_{\mathrm{exp}}-\tfrac{s_3-s_2}{10}.$$
The defect is exactly $(S_3/S_2)^{1/10}$. **No positive linear combination of any monomial constraint in (i)–(iii) can close this gap** — adding $(E)$, $(F)$, $(j{=}1,l{=}3)$, or any rational-homotopy inequality gives $\lambda=0$ for the new multiplier. This is the precise formal obstruction.

**Why each tool fails.**

(i) For pair $(a,b)$, slicing gives $\mathrm{Vol}(R)\gtrsim S_aS_b(R_1F_0^{ab})^{1/2}$ with $S_aS_b\cdot F_0^{ab}=\mathrm{Vol}(S)\cdot\min(S_a,S_b)$, strictly maximized at $(3,4)$. All other pair slicings are weaker, and absolute-value integration kills the Plücker sign relations that might link different $M_{ij}$.

(ii) Slicing by $f_4$ gives $\Lambda_t\to(S_1,S_2,S_3)$ with $\Dil_2\le 1$, degree 1, but $\Lambda_t$ is not rectangular. The 3D $D_2$ theorem's asymmetric $S_1(S_2S_3)^{1/2}$ exponent requires rectangular widths $(W_1,W_2,W_3)$; for generic level sets you only get the degree bound $\Vol(\Lambda_t)\ge S_1S_2S_3$, integrating back to $\Vol(R)\ge\Vol(S)$. The $S_3^{3/2}$ in $T$ fundamentally needs inherited rectangular widths.

(iii) Massey/DGA invariants in 4D produce integer-exponent inequalities $\prod R_i^{a_i}\ge \prod S_i^{a_i}$. Half-integer $S_3$-powers come only from 2-dilation in dimension 3, i.e., slicing again. So DGA enriches the LP with same-type constraints — no breaking power.

**Two suggestions.**

(a) The missing $(S_3/S_2)^{1/10}$ is genuinely parametric — coupling across $\{Z_y\}$, exactly what (13) extracts. I would not abandon (13); the Manhattan cubical-FF in Round 18 is the right geometric content, and the LP analysis above shows that *any* version of (13) that beats LP-optimum suffices, not just the symmetric one.

(b) **Bankable partial.** Your existing tools already prove
$$\Vol(R)\;\gtrsim\;S_1\,S_2^{3/5}\,S_3^{7/5}\,S_4$$
under both hard-regime hypotheses (combine $(D)$ and $(Sl)$ with multipliers $1/5,2/5$). As far as I know this is new; it recovers the desired bound whenever $S_3\lesssim S_2$ and falls short only by $(S_3/S_2)^{1/10}$ in the extreme regime. Worth stating as a theorem in its own right alongside the reduction to (13).

A hybrid path: any weakened (13) with $(\log L)^C$ slack from PL approximation already breaks the LP obstruction in any quantitative aspect-ratio regime, so partial progress on (13) translates directly into bound improvement.
